summary:
The maximum amount of cortisol is secreted during late gestation. Cortisol levels peak around week 36 and are highest at term due to increased activity of the zona fasciculata under maternal influence via placental transfer of ACTH from mother’s circulation into fetus. This supports option A as correct answer for this MCQ about maximal fetal adrenal output being associated with high cortisol concentrations near delivery time.

The maximum steroid produced by the fetal adrenal gland is cortisol, which peaks toward the end of pregnancy—specifically around weeks 35–40—and reaches its highest level just before birth. This increase occurs because maternal ACTH crosses the placenta and stimulates fetal adrenocortical cells, particularly those in the zona fasciculata, leading to enhanced glucocorticoid synthesis. Thus, cortical steroids like cortisol dominate over other types such as aldosterone or dehydroepiandrosterone sulfate (DHEAS) when considering total adrenal output volume per unit mass.

The maximum steroid produced by the fetal adrenal gland is cortisol, which peaks toward the end of pregnancy—specifically around weeks 35–40—and reaches its highest level just before birth. This increase occurs because maternal ACTH crosses the placenta and stimulates fetal adrenocortical cells, particularly those in the zona fasciculata, leading to enhanced glucocorticoid synthesis. Thus, cortical steroids like cortisol dominate over other types such as aldosterone or dehydroepiandrosterone sulfate (DHEAS) when considering total adrenal output volume per unit mass..
